title: Living out of your car is cool!

transcript.text: Hey, how's it going? This is my first video for a while. I wanted to get back into the weightlifting first and then do the videos.
So I'm taking a day off today because I'm tired.
So, my first video is what I think about living out of your car.
I think it's one of the most important things that we can do as a society is live out of your car.
And I'm serious.
Um, because it costs less, it's easier on the environment.
Uh, Americans like to complain, ooh, we use all the resources.
Well, that's because we have these houses.
And we use all this water when we take our showers and when we do our dishes and when we cook and when we water the lawn.
Um, and then spend all this money on our houses.
All these parts break and you have to always replace them and then you lose stuff.
And then you have to buy more things that you lose and then you find the thing that you lost and then you throw it away because you don't need two of them.
And you don't have the room for it all.
And you buy all this stuff because you have all this room for all your stuff.
And, uh, all the electricity you use with all the lights on all the time.
The fans blowing, the air conditioning, the heater, and this and that.
It's like, uh, you know, and then the trash.
All the trash you produce.
And all the fees and all this stuff.
Uh, that's what, that's how we're using up the resources.
So, if you're going to get all like, ooh, we're killing the world, resources.
Then, you know, do something about it.
Uh, or shut up.
Um, now I'm not the kind of guy who complains without giving a, a, um, a, um, an answer right away.
And my answer is, live out of your car.
Um, I've lived out of my car, uh, for a year, an entire year in college.
And then another semester in college.
Uh, I parked my car right behind the campus and just, and my car never worked.
It was a 71 VW van and, um, literally never worked.
So, I just parked it there and I think most of the time it didn't even work.
I just parked it there on some dirt road and just left it there.
And it was only like a five minute ride from campus.
So, it was pretty cool.
It was like my little house.
And I enjoyed it.
Um, I had my showers at the school gym and, uh, it worked out great.
So, anyways, I'm planning on living out of my car again.
Um, last video I made a couple weeks ago.
Um, before I relapsed back in project mode and stopped making videos and going to the gym and stuff.
Uh, it was about, uh, riding my bike around the world.
Like, bike touring was really cool.
Which is cool.
But, uh, and I was actually going to do that.
I was planning on doing that starting in November.
But, um, since then my car broke down again.
And so, I had to put another $2,500 into it.
Which brings it up to like $13,000 I spent on my car this year.
And like $15,000 in the last couple years.
So, I pretty much don't have any money left to travel.
But, I have a healthy car.
And, um, and it costs a lot less to live out of your car.
So, what I'm going to do is starting in December.
I'm going to, uh, live out of my car.
Um, and, uh, be a bike gypsy.
Um, the beauty of, well, I have a nice car to live in.
It's a 91 VW van.
Um, so I'm just going to go out to Moab, I think, for the winter time.
And just park it.
And, like, you know, like, barely ever drive it.
Like, fill up the gas tank, like, once a month type of a situation.
Um, and live out in the boondocks in the middle of nowhere.
Um, and cook all my own food.
And only eat the basic food.
Like, pasta and rice and beans and tuna and vegetables and fruit.
I have a solar panel on my car.
And I'll give a tour of my car when it gets back out of the shop.
But, uh, it can charge a, um, um, a refrigerator that I have in there.
And it also can work the, my computers and stuff.
So I can be out there in my car, you know, with my light shining.
Because it can charge, like, the battery of my actual car, too.
Because I can connect the two batteries.
Um, so I can, like, be out there and basically have my own house.
And the cool, it's like living in a houseboat in the middle of the ocean.
Like, I have my own house there.
Because I have a pop-up.
I can sleep in the top part and, like, hang out in the bottom.
And, uh, hang out and have my own house with all my fixings, you know.
My food.
And so I won't be spending any money at all.
I won't even go back into town for, like, weeks at a time.
And just, uh, just eat the rice and the really basic cheap food.
And just ride my bike all day long.
I'm gonna lose weight.
I'm gonna drop down from, actually, I'm 190 now.
Because I gain weight from not exercising.
But I'll probably beat around 185 when I start the bike touring.
Uh, I have a big belly right now.
But, uh, so I'm gonna drop down to, like, 155.
So I'll lose, like, 30 pounds.
So I'll document that.
That'll be interesting.
But, anyways, I'm gonna document living out of my car.
Uh, so I can show people how easy it is to live out of your car.
It's not only good for the environment to live out of your car.
Um, but it's also good for, um, for you, for people to live out of their cars.
Um, and I think it's, it's exciting for me to, to, to, to document living out of my car.
Because, uh, it'll show how easy it is.
I loved living out of my car when I was in college.
But I'm gonna love living out of my car when I'm not even having any responsibilities.
Because I was kind of like living out of my car with responsibility in a city.
You know, it's different.
Living out of your car in the middle of nowhere.
I mean, you open up your door and you're, like, you have this huge field to yourself.
You have the whole, everything to yourself.
So, uh, you know, I'll have a beacon in there in case anybody steals my car.
And hopefully nobody will break in the windows and steal all my computers and stuff.
But, um, so anyways, I'll, um, maybe I shouldn't have said that.
Anyways, um, I'll, um, I'll, uh, document that.
And I think living out of your car is amazing because you don't spend any money.
A lot of, the big thing that people, the only valid excuse people have of, like, not fulfilling their dreams,
Not doing what they want to do, not enjoying life, not going on vacation, is money.
Um, but living out of your car, you don't spend any money.
So that, that excuse kind of goes out the window, you know.
And you can kind of be like, all right, shut up, dude.
If you're going to be complaining about money.
And you, you know, ooh, we can't travel.
Well, just, you can, just live out of your car.
Um, and, you know, being a bike gypsy, riding your bike around doesn't cost money either.
You never have to replace your gear, you know, that lasts a pretty long time.
Um, and, but also, you have lots of time.
The other problem with people these days is that you just never have enough time.
The days never last long enough.
Um, people have so many projects going on, so much stuff to do.
They're always like, oh, not enough time.
Well, if you live out of your car, all of a sudden you have, like, tons of time.
All you have is time.
So, uh, so yeah.
Like, I'm going to ride my bike all day and then I'll go home in my car and I'll work on my computers and my little computer shit.
And, uh, I'll have all the time in the world to do all, to realize all of my, uh, computer dreams and realize all of my biking dreams and adventure.
And plus, you don't, you're not fixed, you know.
It's like, oh, I live in Moab.
Yeah, yeah, you do.
Yeah, where?
I live right in the Slick Rock parking lot.
Really?
Oh my God.
You can live wherever you want, literally.
You can move your house wherever you want.
And then you can move it somewhere else.
Like, oh, you know what?
I think I'll go to Canyonlands.
So you've gone down to Canyonlands and you live there out of your house, which is your car.

Um, so anyway, living out of your car is, uh, where it's at.
And, uh, more people should.
Now, on that note, people should also be encouraged, but at least allowed, to live out of their freaking cars in town.
Uh, I got busted.
I was living in my van.
Um, I was, you know, it was actually summer school.
And I was in my van on the school campus and I was sleeping in my van this one night.
Normally I sleep in the trees just to avoid trouble.
But I'll sleep in a van that's not, and these, and these cops bang on my door and they're like, what you doing?
And I was like, oh, yeah, just sleeping.
So they're like, ooh.
So they give me a ticket and I have to show up in, uh, in court.
So I go to court and I thought it was like no biggie.
But the judge is all angry like, what you doing?
Leave me out of your car.
Here's a $40 ticket.
So it's like, no, no, that, that can't be allowed.
You got to allow people.
Legally, you got to allow people to live out of your car.
And if they don't have these laws, then you got to, you got to leave them alone.
Leave the car people alone.
Those are the people who are not using resources.
And if you're bitching and moaning about, oh, the world's going down, then leave the fucking people alone who aren't using up the resources.
Duh.
Well, um, so that kind of pissed me off.
Um, so anyways, you should let people who live, you should also delegate a, uh, a parking lot, um, in every town where people can just park their cars to live out of.
And, uh, you know, I know people are going to be like, oh, but it'll go all to hell.
People will shit in the dirt and steal from each other and it'll be hell.
Well, you got to allow that to happen.
You know, you can't just make a prediction and based on your prediction, not allow it to happen.
You have to allow it to happen.
And if you make, and you know, if you don't want to let that happen, at least you're going to have the story of what happened and you can tell what this happened.
And, you know, it took this long and this many people before these bad things happened.
And, you know, you can't just not allow it to happen just because you have a bad feeling about it.
And, you know, this is, this is what we need.
We need, and plus it teaches people to be disciplined, uh, self-sufficient, um, and, uh, people should be allowed to park in front of people's houses too.
Not more than, you know, one night and without people's permission and stuff.
But, you know, people, if someone knocks on your door and says, oh, can I park in front of your house tonight?
You know, you should be like, yeah, that's cool.
It should be smiled upon to live out of your car is basically what I'm saying.
Anyways, I'm out of time.
